---
title: "Your Python environment"
---

# Your Python environment

::: {.callout-warning}
## Placeholder chapter
Stub. To be drafted via the chapter-writing skill.
:::

## What this chapter will cover

The book is Python only. This chapter sets the floor.

- A working Python 3.11 or newer.
- A package manager — `uv` is the current recommendation; `pip` plus `venv` is the conservative alternative; Anaconda is fine if you already have it.
- The handful of libraries the book leans on: `pandas`, `numpy`, `matplotlib`, `seaborn`, `statsmodels`, `requests`, `pydantic`, plus one LLM provider's SDK.
- Per-project virtual environments — what they are and why we use them.
- A quick "is your Python alive" check.

## Why this matters for AI work

AI assistants are at their best when they can see and run your environment. A clean, reproducible setup is the difference between *"AI fixed my regression"* and *"AI gave me code that runs on its imaginary computer but not yours."*

## A note for textbook readers

If you are reading Békés–Kézdi alongside, the textbook's coding appendix walks through the same Python install in more detail. Use that as your primary reference; this chapter is a checklist rather than a tutorial.

## TODO for the author

- A *minimum viable* setup recipe in five lines.
- One pitfall section: PATH issues on Windows, Apple-Silicon vs. Intel package availability on macOS.
- A "verify" block: a 10-line script that imports everything the book uses and prints versions.
